title: Conan Delivers The State Of The Podcast
topics: whether success has changed Conan's grounded persona
summary: The first installment of the bit, introduced as a mock-official ritual ('every once in a while we like to do a very official state of the podcast'). Frames the segment explicitly as a parody of a State of the Union address, then turns into a genuine conversation about whether the show is staying true to its original values as it has grown.

title: Conan's 2023 State Of The Podcast
summary: Explicitly compares the segment to a presidential State of the Union. Discussion centers on live audience shows (Beacon Theater, an LA theater), staging choices for those live tapings, and reflections on how the show's format keeps expanding.

title: Conan Learns About "Brand Safety Analysis"
summary: Explicitly framed as a State of the Podcast check-in ('we haven't done a state of the podcast in a while'). Adam Sachs (exec, runs the company) is brought in to explain an AI/transcription-based 'brand safety' rating system advertisers use to evaluate the show — the first episode to surface concrete business/advertising metrics rather than just vibes.

metrics.note: Adam Sachs states the show is 5 years in and over 400 episodes deep at this point.
Stated as the show's 'high risk' rating for the obscenity/profanity brand-safety category.
A second category also rated 'high risk' in the same breakdown (category itself unclear from audio).

title: Conan Didn't Want To Have A Video Podcast
summary: Opens explicitly as a State of the Podcast segment. Conan jokes about positioning himself as removed from 'the Commerce of it all' while resisting the show's gradual expansion (more mics, more gear, talk of a full band) — a running tension about whether to lean into a video-podcast format Adam Sachs and team have been pushing toward.

metrics.note: A viewership figure for video episodes is mentioned but the auto-caption text is too garbled to extract a reliable number — needs manual re-check against audio, not safe to state as fact.

title: Sona Explains "BBL Smell" To Conan & Gourley
summary: Opens explicitly as a State of the Podcast check-in with Adam Sachs ('we haven't done a state of the podcast in quite some time'), though it gets sidetracked into an unrelated comedic tangent mid-segment before returning to business. Substantive topic: YouTube overtaking other platforms as where most people consume the show, plus an experiment with full-length video episodes (vs. clips) and anecdotal audience reach (Adam mentions recognition while traveling internationally).

metrics.value: Time's "100 Best Podcasts of All Time"
metrics.note: Conan O'Brien Needs a Friend named on this Time Magazine list.

metrics.value: Hollywood Reporter "44 Most Powerful Players in Podcasting" (2025)
metrics.note: Conan named on this Hollywood Reporter list.

metrics.value: iHeartPodcast Awards — Best Ad Read, won 2020 and 2025
metrics.note: Verified directly against iHeart's official winners pages for both years (Team Coco & Earwolf credited in 2020). Not mentioned in any SOTU transcript — added from external research, won multiple times as the show has long claimed.

metrics.value: iHeartPodcast Awards — Best Comedy Podcast, won 2020
metrics.note: Verified directly against iHeart's official 2020 winners page, same ceremony as the Best Ad Read win that year.

title: Conan Learns About His YouTube Audience
topics: comparison to old late-night TV format
summary: Direct continuation of the 2025-11 segment ('last week we were doing a state of the podcast and it got sidetracked'). Confirms YouTube has become the top platform for the show, with the joke that Conan is effectively 'back to hosting a late night show' again now that video is the dominant format — a notable shift from 2024's resistance to video.

metrics.note: Stated as 'over 9 million subscribers on YouTube' for the show's channel.

title: The State Of The Entertainment Business With Producer Jeff Ross
topics: outsider vs insider framing of show business
summary: A wider-lens departure from prior installments — rather than Adam Sachs reporting on this show's own metrics, longtime producer Jeff Ross is brought on to discuss the broader entertainment industry's condition ('it's tough out there'). Most explicitly tied to the original 'State of the Union' framing device but applied to the industry rather than the podcast specifically.
